name: Bogomils
description: The Bogomils were a Christian dualistic sect that emerged in Bulgaria in the 10th century, founded, according to legend, by the priest Bogomil (meaning 'dear to God' or 'loved by God'). Bogomilism is characterized by a dualistic cosmology, viewing the world as a battleground between good and evil, represented by God and Satan respectively. Proponents believe that the material world is the creation of Satan, while the spiritual world is God's creation. They rejected the Old Testament, the sacraments of the Orthodox Church, icons, and hierarchical structures. Bogomils advocated for a simple life, prayer, and personal interpretation of the Gospels. Some alternative history interpretations link Bogomilism to the Cathars of Southern France, suggesting a direct influence through trade routes and migrations. Others propose that Bogomil beliefs represent a hidden, unbroken lineage of early Christian gnostic thought. Mainstream scholarship views Bogomilism as a syncretic heresy arising from a combination of Paulicianism, local pagan beliefs, and social discontent within Bulgarian society. Historians argue that claims of direct lineage to later European heresies are difficult to prove definitively due to limited historical evidence and the challenges of tracing the spread of ideas across vast distances and time periods. Furthermore, the lack of surviving Bogomil texts makes reconstructing their precise beliefs challenging, relying heavily on polemical accounts written by their opponents, which may be biased and inaccurate.
subtitle: Bulgarian Dualist Heresy and its Legacy

claims.claim: The material world is the creation of Satan.
claims.evidence: Bogomil texts (as interpreted by later scholars) and polemical sources describe a dualistic cosmology.
claims.criticism: The precise nature of Bogomil cosmogony is debated due to the scarcity of original sources. Opponents' accounts may misrepresent their views.

claims.claim: Bogomilism directly influenced the Cathars of Southern France.
claims.evidence: Similarities in dualistic beliefs, rejection of sacraments, and emphasis on simple living.
claims.criticism: While similarities exist, direct transmission is difficult to prove definitively. Parallel development or influence from other sources cannot be ruled out. Evidence is circumstantial.

claims.claim: Bogomilism represents a survival of early Gnostic Christianity.
claims.evidence: Shared elements with Gnostic teachings, such as the rejection of the material world and emphasis on spiritual liberation.
claims.criticism: While some overlap exists, Bogomilism also incorporated unique elements not found in earlier Gnostic traditions. It is more likely a later development influenced by various sources.

keyFigures.name: Cosmas the Priest
keyFigures.role: Author of 'Treatise Against the Bogomils', a key source on their beliefs (though critical)

mythologyConnections: Possible connections to pre-Christian Slavic pagan beliefs regarding good and evil spirits.

timeline.event: Spread of Bogomilism to other parts of Europe, including the Balkans and Southern France (speculated)
Decline of Bogomilism due to persecution and assimilation

sources.author: Dimitri Obolensky
sources.work: The Bogomils: A Study in Balkan Neo-Manichaeism
sources.title: Dimitri Obolensky
